video_title: Ethereum's Boomer Era? Apostates, EF Drama, and Solana Fights Back - The Chopping Block
gist: Dragonfly's Chopping Block hosts (Haseeb, Tarun, Tom) debate Bankless co-founder David Hoffman and ETH/Solana apostate Max Resnick on why ETH the asset has plateaued, the wave of Ethereum Foundation departures, ETH's 'Microsoft of crypto' boomer-utility trajectory, the case for a second/for-profit foundation, and whether Solana or Ethereum wins the $500T tokenization race — with a Solana post-quantum vs Ethereum scaling fight.

insights.claim: David Hoffman (Bankless co-founder) sold his last ETH because the 2019-2020 'ETH is money' thesis has largely played out and he sees it as hard for ETH to be re-rated up or down from here — a capital opportunity-cost decision, not a bear call.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman (co-founder Bankless, Superstate)
insights.so_what: A former top ETH proselytizer rotating fully out signals the marginal long-term ETH bull is capitulating on the 'monetary premium' trade; watch for ETH/BTC continuing to drift.
insights.quote: I think it's going to be hard for ETH to get re-rated up or down in any direction.

insights.claim: ETH faces structural barriers to value capture: layer-two margins run at ~98%, and the 'fat app' thesis is displacing the 'fat protocol' thesis, so the L1 captures little of the value flowing through its ecosystem.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: If L2s keep ~98% of margin and apps capture the rest, ETH-the-token is structurally starved of cash flow — undermining any DCF-style valuation and favoring L2/app tokens over the base asset.
insights.quote: layer two margins are at 98% for example. You know, the fat app thesis is displacing the fat protocol thesis.

insights.claim: Because Ethereum services its ecosystem at cost (nonprofit-like, no take rate), ETH can only be re-rated as 'money' — but that requires strong startup-minded leadership to push it, which David argues the EF lacks.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: The bull case for ETH now rests entirely on a 'monetary premium' narrative that needs an aggressive promoter; absent one, the asset lacks a catalyst.
insights.quote: It services everything to its ecosystem at cost... for Ether the money to be like the thesis be fully realized, it would needed to have maintained like a huge market dominance

insights.claim: David frames ETH as being 'at the end of the sigmoid' — plateauing rather than collapsing — explicitly not a doom call but a low-gradient, no-momentum state.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Reframes the bear case as 'dead money / range-bound' rather than 'going to zero' — relevant for option sellers and pair traders rather than outright shorts.
insights.quote: you're at the end of the the sigmoid. You're not at like a high gradient part. You're like, "Okay, we're plateauing."

themes.theme: Ethereum Foundation exodus and the 'who's in charge' problem

insights.claim: A large 2025-26 wave of senior EF departures (Tomas as co-ED after ~11 months; Barnaby and Tim Beiko as protocol cluster co-leads; Carl Beekhuizen after 7 yrs; Julian Ma; Josh Stark after 7 yrs; Trent Van Epps; Pablo Vourt; Alex Stokes on sabbatical; Dankrad earlier to Tempo) has been read as institutional decay.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi (Dragonfly, host)
insights.so_what: The departing names were the 'aggressive/pro-growth' faction — their exit suggests the EF's internal balance has tilted toward slow, credibly-neutral stewardship, reducing odds of a pro-ETH-price pivot.

insights.claim: Vitalik publicly reframed the EF as explicitly NOT the 'number-go-up org,' said its job is to make Ethereum 'impressive,' noted the EF holds only ~16 basis points of ETH supply, and said other orgs should step up as the central node.
insights.speaker: Haseeb (paraphrasing Vitalik)
insights.so_what: Official disavowal of price-stewardship removes any expectation the EF will market or shill ETH — a structural headwind for the monetary-premium narrative.
insights.quote: the EF is not the number go up org. That is not our job. That has never been our job... our job is to make Ethereum impressive.

insights.claim: The EF's ETH holdings have fallen from ~8.3% of supply to ~16 basis points, and with no revenue it will be forced to keep cutting ('DOGE happening out of necessity') and effectively dissolve over the next few years.
insights.speaker: Haseeb
insights.so_what: A shrinking, revenue-less EF means client/protocol development funding (much routed through EF grants to firms like Nethermind) is at structural risk, raising centralization-of-funding questions.
insights.quote: they went from 8.3% of the ether supply to now down to 16 basis points

insights.claim: Max Resnick argues the EF, after accidentally creating a ~$400B asset, has gone 'totally risk off' — prioritizing not nuking the asset over technical ambition, and views some past upgrades (e.g. EIP-4844) as already value-destructive.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick (ETH/Solana apostate, technologist)
insights.so_what: If leadership is optimizing for capital preservation over technical edge, expect conservative, slow upgrades — bearish for catching the tokenization wave on the L1.
insights.quote: they're basically totally risk off. Preserve the value of the asset as much as they can and not try to take any risk.

insights.claim: David's 'conspiratorial' take: part of the EF's DNA is actively adverse to growth because Vitalik doesn't want the responsibility/baggage of a large Ethereum economy — a psychological resistance to economy-maximizing behavior.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Implies the missing ETH catalyst is cultural/founder-driven, not fixable by org charts — so a leadership 'fix' is unlikely without Vitalik stepping back.
insights.quote: part of the EF DNA is actually adverse to growth and Vitalik truly just doesn't want the responsibility

insights.claim: Tarun's signal for ossification: no new blood — the same aging people at the same events — and Ethereum has bled talent faster than the broader (already shrinking) crypto talent market.
insights.speaker: Tarun Chitra (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: 'Follow the talent' as a leading indicator: declining developer/founder inflow precedes ecosystem decline, a metric to track vs Solana/Tempo.
insights.quote: There's no new blood. That's... the real signal... Ethereum has bled more talent than the already fast slowing crypto talent market

insights.claim: Tarun pushes back that the departures are over-attributed to personalities: since the highs ETH's performance hasn't been that different from Solana's, and the real driver is a broad 'L1 drag' where every L1 is down a lot.
insights.speaker: Tarun Chitra
insights.so_what: Suggests ETH underperformance is a beta/sector story, not idiosyncratic — argues against ETH-specific shorts and for an L1-basket view.
insights.quote: since the highs, it's not performance has not been that different than Solana's... there's just been an L1 drag.

themes.theme: Ethereum as the 'Microsoft of crypto' — boomer utility

insights.claim: Haseeb's framework: Ethereum is the 'Microsoft of crypto' — old, enterprise-beloved, deeply embedded, slow, uncool, never first to integrate, but extremely valuable and not going anywhere — likely to succeed despite mediocre execution (a Steve Ballmer era).
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: Reframes ETH as a durable, Lindy 'utility' holding rather than a growth asset — supports a low-conviction long for survivability/trust premium, not for outperformance.
insights.quote: Ethereum has become the Microsoft of crypto... it's very safe. It moves very slowly. It's kind of lame... but it's not going anywhere and it's really valuable.

insights.claim: Haseeb's core valuation bet: the long-run blockchain premium accrues not to performance or deal-count but to being the biggest, most-trusted chain — the dimension Ethereum dominates.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: If 'trust/Lindy' is the moat, ETH's incumbency outweighs Solana's speed — a bull thesis for ETH as settlement layer for high-value assets.
insights.quote: The thing that they're competing on is just being the biggest, most trusted chain. And I... think that's where the premium is going to come from over the long run.

insights.claim: Tarun/Tom argue 'dApps as the final story' and number-go-up messaging is fundamentally uninspiring to young builders, so even if ETH ripped back to $5,000 it wouldn't attract new entrepreneurs.
insights.speaker: Tarun Chitra / Tom
insights.so_what: Price recovery alone won't fix the talent-pipeline problem; bullish ETH narratives that rely on 'price fixes everything' are flawed.
insights.quote: dApps are the final story... such a... uninspiring story to someone who's 19... You're selling the olds. You're not selling the youngs.

insights.claim: The bear-on-multiples view: if Ethereum's users become businesses with low equity/income ratios and PE ratios of ~6, ETH compresses to a DTCC-like utility multiple rather than a 'future of the internet' growth multiple.
insights.speaker: Tarun Chitra
insights.so_what: Frames the valuation ceiling: being the rails for low-margin enterprises caps ETH's multiple — a quantitative anchor for the 'utility, not equity' thesis.
insights.quote: you're kind of in established boring business. You're a utility at that point.

insights.claim: David counters that the EF has little explanatory power for ETH's price: despite a week of EF defections and his public ETH sale, ETH barely moved — so 'crypto Twitter intelligentsia' sentiment is not where the marginal buyer is.
insights.speaker: Haseeb / David
insights.so_what: If the marginal ETH buyer ignores CT sentiment (possibly pricing other things like Venice), then ETH-bearish social narratives may be poor short signals.
insights.quote: Twitter is not pricing ETH... whatever Twitter thinks about ETH is just not where the marginal buyer is coming from.

insights.claim: The Microsoft analogy implies an eventual Satya Nadella-style renaissance — but the panel agrees there is no equivalent talent pipeline inside Ethereum, since there's no for-profit ladder to groom and pay a future leader.
insights.speaker: Tom / Haseeb
insights.so_what: The 'it'll recover like Microsoft did' bull case has a hole: no internal career structure to produce the turnaround leader, weakening the long-term ETH bull thesis.
insights.quote: There is no Satya Nadella joining the ranks of Ethereum core developers today who will be ready to assume the reins in five years

themes.theme: Second foundation / for-profit advocate for ETH

insights.claim: People at the EF are actively working on a 'second foundation' — a for-profit, startup-minded, shareholder-interest entity (akin to a revived Enterprise Ethereum Alliance) — to be the 'number-go-up' advocate the EF refuses to be.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Watch for the launch of a for-profit ETH advocacy org; its legitimacy (EF endorsement + Bitmain backing) could become the catalyst the ETH-money thesis needs.
insights.quote: what's missing is just like a for-profit startup-minded entity who wants to like be a rallying cry for shareholder interest.

insights.claim: A second foundation likely fails on its own because institutional counterparties only meet 'whoever is in charge of EF #1' — moral authority and the head-of-EF title are non-transferable (the 'soybean deal' / China-trip analogy).
insights.speaker: Max Resnick / Haseeb
insights.so_what: Casts doubt on the second-foundation fix; the real BD power stays with the EF's title-holder, so spinning out adoption can't easily work.
insights.quote: people want to be meeting with the person who's in charge... I want to talk to whoever's at of Ethereum Foundation 1 cuz that's where the real power is.

insights.claim: David's fear: Vitalik's outsized sway means he can't simultaneously run Ethereum and credibly empower a differently-minded for-profit entity (the Bob Iger/Disney board-control parallel — real power sits with whoever holds the board seats, i.e. Vitalik and Aya).
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Governance concentration is the binding constraint on any pro-growth pivot; ETH catalysts require Vitalik to actively cede control, which the panel doubts.
insights.quote: it would require Vitalik not having absolute governance over whoever this person is.

insights.claim: The panel's most-likely positive scenario for ETH: Bitmain takes a far more activist role and pairs with the forthcoming second foundation to provide the pro-growth muscle the EF lacks.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman / Haseeb
insights.so_what: Bitmain (described as a ~$10B-ish entity) becoming an ETH activist is a concrete, watchable catalyst — track its public stance and capital commitments.
insights.quote: Bitmain takes a far more activist role and works well and pairs well with this second foundation that's on its way.

insights.claim: A precedent exists for self-organizing spiritual leadership: Michael Saylor became 'Mr. Bitcoin' purely via financial gravitational force, showing a dominant capital holder can become a chain's de facto promoter without official sanction.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: Implies a large ETH treasury/whale could spontaneously fill the leadership vacuum — a reason not to fully write off the ETH-money narrative.
insights.quote: Saylor has a stronger gravitational force than anybody else in Bitcoin... the idea that like this can't happen on its own... is just insufficiently imaginative.

themes.theme: Competitive landscape: Solana, Base, Tempo, stablecoin chains

insights.claim: Haseeb argues the feared 'ETH killers' have all underperformed expectations: a year and a half on, neither Base nor Solana nor Tempo has eaten Ethereum's lunch, and Ethereum still holds the vast majority of TVL.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: The 'ETH is dead' competitive narrative has repeatedly failed to play out — a reason to fade extreme ETH-bearishness on competitive grounds.
insights.quote: do you see Base eating Ethereum alive? Do you see Solana eating Ethereum alive?... all those things didn't really happen. Ethereum is still massive, still has vast majority of the TVL.

insights.claim: As a VC, Haseeb says Solana's developer/founder market share peaked ~18 months ago and has clearly crested — though new builders aren't going to Ethereum mainnet either, but to EVM-compatible alternatives.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: Counters the 'Solana is winning devs' narrative; founder flow is fragmenting to EVM L2s/alt-chains, not consolidating on either leader — bearish for a clean ETH-vs-SOL binary.
insights.quote: Solana was all-time high probably a year and a half ago... it's clearly crested in terms of the market share of people building on Solana.

insights.claim: Max counters that on Gauntlet's developer metrics Solana has been winning vs Ethereum (and Base has done poorly), creating an explicit data disagreement on Solana's developer trajectory.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: The developer-share data is genuinely contested between practitioners — a metric to independently verify rather than take on narrative.
insights.quote: on the developer side, Ethereum has been losing to Solana over the past... Solana has been doing pretty well on our developer metrics.

insights.claim: The stablecoin-chain threat to ETH has largely fizzled: Stable, Plasma, Arc and Taproot mostly fell flat and aren't regaining momentum, so stablecoin and tokenized-RWA activity isn't migrating off Ethereum soon.
insights.speaker: Tom
insights.so_what: Removes a near-term bear catalyst (stablecoin-chain disintermediation), supporting ETH's incumbency in stablecoin/RWA settlement.
insights.quote: we had stable and plasma and arc and taproot. And those have really mostly fallen flat.

insights.claim: Tom names Hyperliquid as a 'real' threat on the DeFi side — distinct from the failed stablecoin chains — singling it out as the credible competitive risk to watch.
insights.speaker: Tom
insights.so_what: Hyperliquid flagged as the one genuinely threatening DeFi competitor; warrants positioning attention vs other 'fake-metric' rivals.
insights.quote: the hyper liquid threat... is kind of real

insights.claim: Etherealize is cited as the model for breaking EF orthodoxy — an unapologetically enterprise-/Wall-Street-focused org willing to make decentralization compromises, which gains share automatically as the EF weakens.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: As the EF recedes, enterprise-aligned orgs like Etherealize become a larger share of Ethereum's footprint — a structural tilt toward 'weak crypto' adoption rails.
insights.quote: Etherealize is we are for enterprises, period, full stop, end of story... We're here to actually get Wall Street on chain.

themes.theme: Strong crypto vs weak crypto regime shift

insights.claim: David's 'strong vs weak crypto' framework: 'strong crypto' (DeFi, NFTs, cypherpunk, rewriting finance) — Ethereum's native habitat — is at its weakest, while 'weak crypto' (blockchain as a back-end efficiency upgrade for big financial institutions) is thriving.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: A regime shift away from ETH's core 'strong crypto' use case toward enterprise rails reframes which assets/chains capture the next cycle — favoring infrastructure plays over speculative DeFi.
insights.quote: Weak crypto is doing really well right now. Strong crypto, not so well. And... DeFi especially, which is Ethereum's core driver, is the weakest it's ever been

insights.claim: David ties ETH's peak monetary moment to the COVID lockdown (everyone stuck inside, forced online, money loose), implying ETH's 'money' premium was a special macro condition unlikely to repeat at the same scale.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: If ETH's monetary premium was a one-off macro artifact, betting on a repeat 'ETH is money' regime is betting on conditions that may not recur.
insights.quote: when was ETH the most money it ever was? It was when everyone was stuck inside during COVID under lockdown

insights.claim: Haseeb disputes that DeFi is at its weakest ever — citing FTX's 2022 collapse as a far weaker moment, with TVL, trading volumes and on-chain flows having ground up robustly over multiple years despite the last 6 months down.
insights.speaker: Haseeb Qureshi
insights.so_what: Direct rebuttal to the DeFi-decline narrative; if TVL/volumes are structurally higher than post-FTX, ETH's core driver is healthier than headlines suggest.
insights.quote: we were FTX exploded like 3 years ago... TVL like on chain and DeFi look stronger if you zoom out than they have in a long long time.

insights.claim: Max dismisses headline stablecoin and chain volume metrics (on both Ethereum and Solana) as 'fake' — largely exchange-to-exchange deposit-address transfers, not genuine economic activity; only AUM is real.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: If on-chain volume metrics are inflated wash/transfer flow, valuation and dominance comparisons based on them are unreliable — anchor on AUM instead.
insights.quote: Stable coin volume, fake. It's just it's like totally fake... it's people transferring money from one exchange deposit address to another.

insights.claim: Max's Solana bull case: ~$500T of global securities are coming on-chain, regulators (SEC) want it on real decentralized chains (not centralized systems), leaving effectively two viable venues — Ethereum or Solana — and Ethereum lacks the scale to host the global financial system today.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: Positions Solana as the scalable winner of the tokenization wave if Ethereum can't scale its L1 in time — the core ETH-vs-SOL trade thesis.
insights.quote: there are basically two options in terms of public blockchains that you would want to go on... Ethereum and Solana, and Ethereum doesn't have enough scale to accommodate the global financial system at this time.

insights.claim: Max sees a major missed opportunity: with regulatory clarity arriving, no one is aggressively lobbying Washington, the DTCC and transfer agents to actually push securities on-chain — a coordination/BD gap, not a tech gap.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: Whoever does the regulatory/BD legwork ('pound on the door') could capture the tokenization flow — an execution edge available to either chain's advocates.
insights.quote: it's a really big missed opportunity to not have people pounding down the door in Washington and at the DTCC and at the transfer agents

insights.claim: David's risk scenario: if everyone is ready to put assets on-chain (DTCC, etc.) but Ethereum says it won't be scaled enough until ~2031, that flow defects to Solana, Hyperledger or elsewhere — a genuine risk he acknowledges.
insights.speaker: Haseeb (scenario) / David (concession)
insights.so_what: Scaling timeline is the pivotal variable: if ETH L1 lags institutional demand, it permanently cedes the RWA flow — the single biggest bear catalyst named.
insights.quote: based on our current rate of increasing the gas limit, we'll be ready for you in... 2031... And then they just choose to go somewhere else, like Solana or Hyperledger

insights.claim: It's not even given that institutions use public chains at all: Nasdaq and NYSE are building their own internal blockchains, and DTCC (charging ~6 cents/transaction, ~$1.5B/yr revenue) may run its own chain rather than picking Ethereum or Solana.
insights.speaker: Tom / Haseeb
insights.so_what: The entire ETH-vs-SOL tokenization thesis may be moot if incumbents run private chains — a tail risk that undercuts both bull cases.
insights.quote: Nasdaq and the New York Stock Exchange both are doing their own internal blockchains... Why are is it coming on the public chain? Do we even know that?

insights.claim: Even the DTCC's own economics (~$1.5B/yr revenue) wouldn't justify Ethereum's valuation — implying the tokenization narrative alone can't underwrite ETH's market cap, Haseeb concedes.
insights.speaker: Tom / Haseeb
insights.so_what: Quantifies the gap between RWA fee potential and ETH's valuation — a sober anchor against over-pricing the tokenization story.
insights.quote: they charge 6 cents a transaction at the DTCC... and they make 1.5 billion dollars a year... But that's still not really enough to justify Ethereum's valuation, right?

insights.claim: David's 'make it up on scale' thesis: blockchains should forego fees today (vs the ~2% 'highway robbery' of 2021, which only worked due to no interoperability and trapped capital) to win volume, then earn a tiny rate on a huge base — and if scale never comes, the whole blockchain thesis was wrong.
insights.speaker: David Hoffman
insights.so_what: Justifies near-zero L1 take rates as strategy, not weakness — but explicitly makes ETH's value contingent on blockchains becoming 'one of the main things in the world' within ~15 years.
insights.quote: it's a classic you make it up on scale... If blockchains don't get there, then we kind of lost. We were kind of wrong about the thesis

insights.claim: Tom Lee's framing that 'stablecoins are the ChatGPT of crypto' (and stablecoins live on Ethereum) is mocked by the panel as a pure-adoption, non-technical comparison — though they concede it coincided with ETH running to all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Tom / Haseeb
insights.so_what: The dominant retail/institutional bull narrative for ETH is now 'stablecoins on Ethereum' — note it as the marketing line moving price even if technically thin.
insights.quote: Stable coins are the chat GPT of crypto.

themes.theme: Quantum threat: Solana vs Ethereum architecture

insights.claim: Max argues post-quantum is a far smaller problem for Solana than Ethereum: Solana has abundant bandwidth (100 Gbit NICs) and will use a smaller signature scheme (Falcon, ~8x smaller), whereas Ethereum's solo-staker Raspberry-Pi target plus ~4,000-byte signatures per transaction/vote chokes its already low-bandwidth network.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: Frames quantum as an asymmetric architectural risk that hurts Ethereum's decentralization model more than Solana's — a long-dated differentiator for the ETH-vs-SOL debate.
insights.quote: For Ethereum, they have a solo staker Raspberry Pi that can only do so many bits per second... we're going to use a smaller signature, Falcon... eight times smaller than that and we have way more bandwidth.

insights.claim: Max claims Solana's post-quantum design (Falcon, ~600-byte signatures vs 32 today) costs only a ~2x constant factor in bandwidth — offset by bandwidth doubling every ~2 years — with no end-user performance impact and still reaching ~1M TPS; Solana won't flip the switch until quantum is a real signal, likely within 5 years.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: If credible, the quantum migration is a non-event for Solana performance — neutralizing a feared 'obvious bad thing' overhang on SOL.
insights.quote: it's a factor of like two. It's like ultimately a constant factor and bandwidth is increasing by factor of two every two years... maybe it will change the timeline by two years

insights.claim: Max contends Ethereum's million-validator design (a '10-year-old poor architecture decision') is a post-quantum liability — a MacBook can only aggregate ~800 signatures/second, so aggregating a million validator signatures requires heavy compute.
insights.speaker: Max Resnick
insights.so_what: Pinpoints Ethereum's large validator set as a specific, quantifiable scaling/quantum bottleneck — a technical bear point distinct from leadership/narrative concerns.
insights.quote: they have a million validators on ETH cuz they shot themselves in the foot with a very poor architecture decision like 10 years ago. And a MacBook can... aggregate 800 signatures in 1 second.

insights.claim: Tarun is sanguine on quantum compute cost: once a post-quantum standard is chosen, CPU vendors will optimize it to a single instruction (as happened when Intel's SHA SIMD opcode made SHA complaints vanish), though he concedes quantum may kill the cheap Raspberry-Pi validator.
insights.speaker: Tarun Chitra
insights.so_what: Suggests the compute side of quantum migration is a solved-by-progress problem; the real casualty is ultra-cheap home staking, a decentralization (not performance) concern.
insights.quote: once a standard is chosen, like every CPU provider is just going to make it a one-click... the way that like SHA was optimized away
